title: Novo Nordisk trial shows promise for teen diabetes treatment
summary: Novo Nordisk has reported positive results from a clinical trial evaluating its oral semaglutide treatment in children with type 2 diabetes.
thbAction: Map the news to THB's provider CRM for referral growth, patient lifecycle engagement, and multi-facility relationship management across hospital and provider networks.
contentHtml:
Skip to content

Novo Nordisk has reported positive results from a late-stage clinical trial evaluating its oral semaglutide treatment in children and adolescents with type 2 diabetes, marking a potential shift in how the condition is managed in younger patients.

The phase 3a PIONEER TEENS trial assessed the drug in individuals aged 10 to 17 and found that it delivered a statistically significant reduction in HbA1c levels compared to placebo. The treatment also demonstrated a safety profile consistent with previous semaglutide studies conducted in adults.

The findings come at a time when type 2 diabetes is becoming increasingly prevalent among younger populations. In 2021, an estimated 14.6 million adolescents were living with the condition globally, a figure projected to rise to 20.9 million by 2030.

The disease in youth is often more aggressive than in adults and is associated with a higher risk of complications earlier in life.

Current treatment options remain limited. Standard first-line therapies include metformin and insulin, but both present challenges. Metformin is estimated to fail in achieving adequate glycaemic control in around half of adolescent patients, while insulin treatment can lead to side effects such as weight gain and hypoglycaemia.

Novo Nordisk said oral semaglutide could help address this gap, particularly for patients who may struggle with injectable therapies. The drug is already approved for use in adults under the brand name Rybelsus and is part of the broader GLP-1 receptor agonist class, which has been widely adopted for diabetes management.

Dr Motaz Azzam, senior paediatric endocrinology fellow at Al Jalila Children’s Hospital, said the introduction of an oral option could significantly improve treatment adherence among younger patients.

“For many children and adolescents, particularly those who find injections difficult, a pill-based option could lower the barrier to treatment and support more consistent use,” he said.

However, he cautioned that medication alone is not sufficient to manage the condition effectively. “This is not a quick fix. Treatment must still be supported by lifestyle changes, family involvement and regular medical follow-up.”

Pending regulatory approvals, oral semaglutide could become the first oral GLP-1 therapy available for children and adolescents with type 2 diabetes, offering clinicians an additional tool to manage a growing global health challenge.
